artifacts.content:
Erin,

Long day and I’m still wired, so I figured I’d write this out before I forget half of it. I spent most of the afternoon tightening up the staff report for the Feb. 2 council meeting – the “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Street Department” item. On paper it’s just a straight CIP replacement for Unit #24, but you and I both know it’s about everything that’s piled up the last couple winters.

I kept thinking about that December 9, 2024 storm when Unit 24 died by Brainerd High and Jake sat there for almost three hours in that brutal windchill while I was on the phone with Mark Olson and fielding East River Road complaints. Then my mind jumped to Dad’s January 5, 2024 hip-replacement aftermath at Cuyuna and how the hydraulic line blew on County Road 48, leaving their place at 612 buried for nearly 14 hours so he had to cancel PT with Megan Schultz. Those are the nights that stick.

And of course there’s my own stuff – that November 18, 2023 Oak Street breakdown near St. Joe’s when Dan Miller was out on FMLA and I pushed through a 16‑hour shift, only to end up at Essentia the next morning with chest pain. Dr. Peters flat-out told me it was stress and fatigue. I didn’t put any of that in the memo, but it’s in the back of my mind when I say “reliability and safety.”

Anyway, enough shop talk. How did Emily do at band tonight? Did she decide on flute or stick with clarinet for the spring concert? I should be home around 6:30 tomorrow unless we get surprise snow.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Truck item + all the baggage behind it

artifacts.content:
Sarah,

Catching up between meetings. I’ve been buried in this plow truck replacement push and it’s stirring up all the family/work overlap stuff.

I keep flashing back to Dad’s fall outside the Maple Ridge duplex during that nasty cold snap in late December 2024, when North Memorial hauled him to Essentia–St. Joe’s with the suspected hip fracture. Unit 24 was sitting at Nuss in Sauk Rapids, so we had zero backup tandem and the northeast quadrant got behind. One of their neighbors called City Hall, made sure to mention that their street was still a mess “even though the Public Works Director’s own parents live here.” That one stung.

Then there’s Dad’s knee replacement coming up March 3 at Essentia–St. Joe’s. I’ve already talked with Dan Peterson about putting in some overtime in February on Unit 24 at the Laurel Street garage so it’s less likely to die right when I’m supposed to be driving Dad to post‑op visits. It feels ridiculous to schedule OT around surgeries, but that’s where we’re at.

On top of that, I’m still thinking about that January 12, 2024 blizzard when Unit 24’s hydraulic line blew and I had to bail on watching Liam at your place on South 7th while you were on a 12‑hour ER shift. I hate that the truck’s condition is dictating whether I can help with family stuff.

How’s Dad actually feeling about the knee surgery? He downplays everything with me. And how’s Liam’s hockey going – still living at the rink? We should pick a Sunday after church to all do chili at our place again.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Dad, the truck, and juggling everything

artifacts.content:
Erin,

I know we’ve talked this to death, but I had another sit‑down with Finance Director Laura Peterson this morning about the Equipment Fund and Unit 24. We went over the $27,000‑plus we’ve sunk into that truck in the last two years and why I’m so insistent on sticking with the 2026 replacement.

I didn’t say it outright this time, but in the back of my head I kept hearing myself blurting to her last March that we had to take that $15,000 home equity line at Brainerd Lakes Federal Credit Union in January 2024 just to finish the roof and clear Jacob’s 2023 knee surgery bill from the Orthopaedic Center of St. Cloud. That conversation is part of why I’m hypersensitive about anyone implying I’m casual with money – at home or at work.

Same with when I briefly toyed with leasing last April and asked Mark Olson if there was any way to structure payments lighter in 2026–27 because of Jacob’s $18,500 concussion bill from Brainerd Lakes Orthopaedic & Sports Medicine. I walked that back pretty fast; family finances shouldn’t be driving city policy.

All of that is swirling while I’m trying to present this truck purchase as a straightforward, budgeted, state‑contract thing. On paper it is. Emotionally, it’s tangled up with our HELOC, medical stuff, and that IRS installment plan we set up last March for the payroll tax mess. I keep reminding myself council doesn’t need or want that backstory.

On a lighter note, what’s the plan for dinner? If you’re wiped, I can grab tacos on the way home.

– Mike

metadata.subject: Money nerves tied up with this truck

artifacts.content:
Personal note – Unit 24 / stress / health

I need this in one place for myself, not for anyone else.

Every time I start drafting talking points about “reliability and safety,” what I’m really thinking about is that March 2024 storm when I pulled a 14‑hour overtime shift and then sat in Dr. Nelson’s exam room at Essentia–Brainerd while he told me I was on the edge of needing stress leave. Same pattern as November 18, 2023, when Unit 24 quit on Oak by St. Joe’s, Dan Miller was out on FMLA, and I ended up at Essentia the next morning with chest pain and a blood pressure spike that Dr. Amanda Peters tied directly to fatigue.

Add in the February 2024 ice storm where the guys were doing back‑to‑back 14–16 hour shifts and a couple of them ended up with Dr. Karen Schultz at the Cuyuna Lakes EAP office in Crosby, telling her they were burned out. Linda and I have the email trail about that.

And then the January 10, 2026 ice event – sending Dan Schultz home from the SE 13th Street garage because he clearly wasn’t okay, likely tied to his alcohol treatment at Essentia, while Mom and Dad sat on Maple Ridge wondering if a truck was ever coming.

None of that belongs in a council packet, but it’s the reality behind why I’m pushing so hard to get this truck replaced on schedule and, selfishly, before my own hernia surgery and FMLA in late January 2026.

– MML

Mom,

Just wanted to update you on a few things before the weekend. First, yes, the snowplow truck issue is still hanging over everything. We had that near‑miss on Laurel Street in early November when Unit 24 stalled spreading salt by Brainerd High. The resident who almost slid into the back of it sent a pretty heated email, copied the City Administrator, and even mentioned knowing where Jacob and Emily walk home and that they’d “make noise” with the Brainerd Dispatch if “my junk truck” caused a crash. That one shook me more than I let on.

It brought me right back to Dad’s fall outside the Maple Ridge duplex during the late December 2024 cold snap, when North Memorial took him to Essentia–St. Joe’s and I had to leave the shop for over an hour. With Unit 24 at Nuss in Sauk Rapids, we had no backup tandem and the northeast quadrant lagged; of course his neighbor called to complain and made sure to mention I’m the Public Works Director.

I’m trying hard not to let any of this spill over into how I treat the operators. Mark Peterson, for example, is still on that last‑chance agreement after his 2023 back injury workers’ comp claim, and in March 2025 when the underbody scraper failed at 6th NE and Oak, we had to call him in for overtime even though he was close to 60 hours. That’s exactly the kind of thing that keeps me up at night.

On happier topics, Emily’s band concert is next Thursday at Forestview – 7 p.m. if you and Dad feel up to it. We can pick you up so you don’t have to worry about parking.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Quick update – truck headaches & family stuff

artifacts.content:
Event: Council Meeting – Plow Truck Authorization & Prep

Time: Monday, February 2, 2026, 5:30–9:00 p.m.
Location: Brainerd City Hall – Council Chambers
Attendees: Michael Larson; Mayor; City Administrator; City Council; Finance Director Laura Peterson; interested public

Notes:
– Agenda item: “Authorization to Purchase Replacement Snowplow Truck for Street Department” (main agenda; P&F Committee; not consent).
– Presenter: Michael M. Larson, P.E., Public Works Director/City Engineer. Estimated time: 10 minutes, but expect questions on Equipment Fund balance, $298,450.00 total cost (state cooperative contract with Northland Truck Equipment – chassis, stainless box, plow/wing, underbody scraper, spreader, pre‑wet, tax, delivery).
– Be ready if someone raises the October 2024 anonymous ethics complaint to the Minnesota Board of Engineering and Land Surveying about alleged favoritism toward Northland. Keep answers factual, emphasize state contract process, Finance review, prior satisfactory performance.
– Keep personal stuff out of it: do NOT mention IRS installment agreement from March 2024, HELOC, or any of Erin/Jacob/Emily’s medical history.
– Coordinate with shop beforehand so operators are covered; I may be in and out for calls if weather turns.
– Remember surgery/FMLA timing at Essentia–St. Joe’s later in January; aim to have specs and purchase order ready so setup can proceed even if I’m in and out.

Reminder: Bring printed copy of recommended motion and CIP page.

Hey Mark,

I’m stealing a page from your book and venting to someone outside my own city limits.

We’ve got another tandem‑axle plow purchase teed up with Northland Truck Equipment for 2026, around $298,450 all in under the state cooperative contract. On the merits it’s solid – spec matches our 2019 and 2021 units, Equipment Fund has it budgeted, and the old 2007 Sterling (Unit 24) is a rust bucket that’s eaten over $27k in major repairs the last two years.

But ever since that anonymous ethics complaint hit the Minnesota Board of Engineering and Land Surveying in October 2024 accusing me of favoring Northland on prior bids, I’ve been second‑guessing every email I write. The Board hasn’t said much beyond the standard “under review,” but the timing sucks with another Northland truck on the horizon.

Complicating things, I already had that awkward one‑on‑one at City Hall on January 8, 2025 with Council Member Sarah Jensen where we talked through a preliminary $298,500 quote and her worries about how Tom’s layoff from the Brainerd Equipment dealership might look politically. I’ve been very careful to keep that out of any written justification, but it still feels messy.

Meanwhile, the operators are fried from back‑to‑back storms and the February 2024 ice event that drove a couple of them to Dr. Karen Schultz at the Cuyuna Lakes EAP. They just want a truck that starts.

How do you walk the line between sticking with a vendor that’s delivered for you and avoiding the appearance of coziness? Any war stories welcome.

– Mike
